Long ago, the world was bathed in an unyielding, blinding light. The Sun God, Solis, loved the earth so much that he never closed his eyes, keeping the land in a state of eternal, scorching noon. The creatures of the earth grew exhausted, unable to rest or hide from the perpetual glare.

Seeing their suffering, a small, clever raven named Corvus decided to help. Corvus flew higher than any bird had dared, soaring directly toward the blinding face of Solis. The heat singed his feathers, turning them from pure white to a charred, iridescent black. When Corvus finally reached the Sun God, he didn't attack; instead, he unfolded his massive, scorched wings, momentarily blocking the light.

For the first time, a cool patch of darkness fell upon the earth. Solis, surprised by the bird's bravery and the dark beauty of the contrast, struck a deal. He would allow pieces of Corvus's dark feathers to attach to every creature and object, stretching out behind them to provide a sliver of personal respite from the light.

Thus, shadows were born, tethered to us forever—a gift from the raven who dared to eclipse the sun.

Myth: Long after Corvus the raven earned his scorched black feathers and gifted the world shadows, the earth finally found rest. But with the Sun God Solis closing his eyes at last, a new problem arose: the night was absolute, a suffocating void of pitch-black. In this darkness dwelt the Moon, a pale, cold spirit who wandered unseen and desperately lonely.

Corvus, now a creature of both light and shadow, heard the Moon's silent weeping. Determined to help once more, the brave raven flew back toward the resting Solis. Very carefully, he plucked a mouthful of glowing, golden embers from the Sun God’s eternal hearth.

Corvus took flight across the vast expanse of the night sky. But the embers were searingly hot. As they burned his beak, he was forced to drop them, one by one. Wherever an ember fell, it embedded itself in the dark velvet of the heavens, burning eternally.

When Corvus finally reached the Moon, he had only one large ember left. He placed it gently upon her surface, igniting her with a soft, silver radiance. Thus, the stars were scattered, and the Moon glowed, ensuring the night would never be truly dark again.
